rationale: Both candidates correctly validate the core fix (codes.audio read/write match), but the ground truth's real substance is the reviewer's note that the containment fallback now applies unconditionally to all callers while the docstring/comment still claims 'strict behaviour preserved... other tests unaffected.' Y directly surfaces this (unconditional global loosening of _assert_transcript_matches, with a concrete failure example: 'hello' vs 'hello world goodbye') and adds a second sharp, grounded finding (the min() short_text check being nearly always true) — both precise and actionable. X spends most of its output on validation/sweep notes and raises different, lower-value minor nits (missing docstring, duplicate helper suggestion, extra test coverage) that don't overlap with the ground truth's actual doc-staleness and legacy-producer concerns, and includes irrelevant boilerplate (skill candidates) diluting focus. Neither has a LATENT GAP CHECK section, so gap_hit is false for both.
